verbatim_text: And finally, after 50 steps, we have a solution that beats the best tool. That's it. Very simple. Now, here's the part that's slightly more complicated, is that you see that the distribution are getting wider and wider, right? They're all getting wider at least than the initial set. Why is that? First of all, that's sort of desired, right? In classical RL, you normally end up classing to just the best solution, because in classical RL, you want to output a policy that has good reward on that. But in this case, we really care about the tail over here. As long as we have a good solution in the tail, it doesn't matter what the rest of the distribution looks like. So really, we have an extreme case of exploration, and in our RL objective, we put the exploration into the objective instead of doing the normal strategies such as epsilon-green. Or in detail, to make the same work in practice. Can I have a question? Can you go a bit deeper into the difference between what you're doing and reinforcement learning?
